title: Powsin

infobox.value: (Polish pronunciation: [[ˈpɔf.ɕin]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish")) is a neighbourhood, and an area of the [City Information System](/wiki/City_Information_System_(Warsaw) "City Information System (Warsaw)"), in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), Poland, within the district of [Wilanów](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w "Wilanów"). It is a residential area with single-family housing.
Barbara Petrozolin-Skowrońska (editor): *Encyklopedia Warszawy*. Warsaw: Wydawnictwo Naukowe PWN, Warszawa, 1994, p. 427. ISBN 83-01-08836-2. (in Polish)
Barbara Czopek-Kopciuch, Kazimierz Rymut (editors): *Nazwy miejscowe Polski: L-Mą*. Warsaw: Wydawnictwo Instytutu Jezyka Polskiego PAN, 2011. (in Polish)
Franciszek Galiński: *Gawędy o Warszawie*. Warsaw: Instytut Wydawniczy Biblioteka Polska, 1939, p. 239. (in Polish)

**Powsin** (Polish pronunciation: [[ˈpɔf.ɕin]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish")) is a neighbourhood, and an area of the [City Information System](/wiki/City_Information_System_(Warsaw) "City Information System (Warsaw)"), in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), Poland, within the district of [Wilanów](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w "Wilanów"). It is a residential area with single-family housing.

Powiat was founded in the 13th century as a farm community. Throughout the centuries there also developed settlements of [Kępa Latoszkowa](/wiki/K%C4%99pa_Latoszkowa "Kępa Latoszkowa"), [Latoszki](/wiki/Latoszki "Latoszki"), [Lisy](/wiki/Lisy,_Warsaw "Lisy, Warsaw"), and [Zamość](/w/index.php?title=Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87,_Warsaw&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zamość, Warsaw (page does not exist)"). In the 18th century, there was constructed the St. Elizabeth Church. The area was incorporated into Warsaw in 1951.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Powsin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The oldest known records of Powsin, then known as *Powsino* date to 13th century. It was a farming community owned by Bogusza Miecławic of the clan of [Doliwa](/wiki/Doliwa_coat_of_arms "Doliwa coat of arms"), the [voivode](/wiki/Voivode "Voivode") of [Łęczyca](/wiki/%C5%81%C4%99czyca_Voivodeship "Łęczyca Voivodeship"). In 1258, he gave its ownership to the [St. John the Baptist Archcathedral](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw_Cathedral "Wrocław Cathedral") in [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"), which was later approved by duke [Siemowit I](/wiki/Siemowit_I_of_Masovia "Siemowit I of Masovia"), ruler of the [Duchy of Masovia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Masovia "Duchy of Masovia"). In 1283, bishop Albertus gave the settlement to Mokołaj Ciołek, the [castellan](/wiki/Castellan "Castellan") of [Wizna](/wiki/Wizna "Wizna"), in exchange for Szawłowice near [Gniezno](/wiki/Gniezno "Gniezno"). In 1398, Elżbieta Ciołkowska, widow after Andrzej Ciołek, castellan of [Czersk](/wiki/Czersk,_Masovian_Voivodeship "Czersk, Masovian Voivodeship"), founded in Powsin the construction the St. Andrew the Apostle and St. Elizabeth. In 1410, it became a seat of a new [parish](/wiki/Parish_(Catholic_Church) "Parish (Catholic Church)"). The wooden building most likely was burned down during the [Northern War](/wiki/Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660 "Northern War of 1655–1660") in the 1650s.[[1]](#cite_note-dm-1)

In the 15th century, in the area was also founded the village of [Lisy](/wiki/Lisy,_Warsaw "Lisy, Warsaw"), and in the 16th century, [Latoszki](/wiki/Latoszki "Latoszki").[[2]](#cite_note-lisy-2)[[3]](#cite_note-latoszki-3)

The descendants of the Ciołek family adopted surname Powsiński, and owned the village intol 1677, when it was sold to king [John III Sobieski](/wiki/John_III_Sobieski "John III Sobieski"), ruler of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), whom incorporated it into the [Wilanów Estate](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Estate "Wilanów Estate").[[1]](#cite_note-dm-1)[[4]](#cite_note-ew-4) It was later bought by [Elżbieta Helena Sieniawska](/wiki/El%C5%BCbieta_Helena_Sieniawska "Elżbieta Helena Sieniawska") in 1720s, whom in 1725, founded the construction of the St. Elizabeth Church.[[5]](#cite_note-arch-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Between 1803 and 1815, its [parson](/wiki/Parson "Parson") was [Jan Paweł Woronicz](/wiki/Jan_Pawe%C5%82_Woronicz "Jan Paweł Woronicz"), who would later become the [primate of Poland](/wiki/List_of_archbishops_of_Gniezno_and_primates_of_Poland "List of archbishops of Gniezno and primates of Poland").[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 1720, nearby was also founded a cemetery.[[8]](#cite_note-cemetery-8)

In 1831, in the area was founded the village of [Kępa Latoszkowa](/wiki/K%C4%99pa_Latoszkowa "Kępa Latoszkowa").[[9]](#cite_note-kepa-9) By the 19th century, there was also present [Zamość](/w/index.php?title=Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87,_Warsaw&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zamość, Warsaw (page does not exist)").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Following the [abolition of serfdom](/wiki/Abolition_of_serfdom_in_Poland "Abolition of serfdom in Poland") in 1864, Powsin and surrounding it were incorporated into the [municipality](/wiki/Gmina "Gmina") of [Wilanów](/wiki/Gmina_Wilan%C3%B3w "Gmina Wilanów").[[4]](#cite_note-ew-4)

In 1896, in Powsiny was opened a [narrow-gauge](/wiki/Narrow-gauge_railway "Narrow-gauge railway") railway station of the Wilanów Railway, at the line connecting [Mokotów](/wiki/Mokot%C3%B3w "Mokotów") and [Piaseczno](/wiki/Piaseczno "Piaseczno"), and later extended to [Klarysew](/wiki/Konstancin-Jeziorna "Konstancin-Jeziorna"). On 16 July 1939, a two trains collided with each other in Powsin, resulting in deaths of 10 people, and over 200 being injured.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1938, the Polski Country Club bought a 50-hectare-area to the east of Powsiny, from count [Adam Branicki](/w/index.php?title=Adam_Branicki&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adam Branicki (page does not exist)") to build a [golf course](/wiki/Golf_course "Golf course"). In the 1940s, during the [occupation of Poland](/wiki/Occupation_of_Poland_(1939%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Poland (1939–1945)") in the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it was turned into farmland. In 1947, the land was donated to the city of [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), to establish a recreational area, which became the [Powsin Culture Park](/wiki/Powsin_Culture_Park "Powsin Culture Park").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Currently, it is part of the City Information System area of [Skarpa Powsińska](/wiki/Skarpa_Powsi%C5%84ska "Skarpa Powsińska").[[14]](#cite_note-msiu-14)

On 26 September 1943, during the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [Polish Underground State](/wiki/Polish_Underground_State "Polish Underground State") carried out in Kępa Latoszkowa a portion of the Operation Wilanów, targeting [German](/wiki/German_people "German people") settlers in the village. As a result, four building were burned down, and twelve people assassinated, including three officers of the [Blue Police](/wiki/Blue_Police "Blue Police"), as well as the family and coworkers of August Friedrich Boraun, the local leader of the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1945, in Przyczółkowa Street was opened the Powsin Warsaw Insurgents Cemetery, the oldest necropolis dedicated to the veterans of the [Warsaw Uprising](/wiki/Warsaw_Uprising "Warsaw Uprising").[[16]](#cite_note-pap-16)

On 15 May 1951, the Powsiny and surrounding it area were incorporated into Warsaw, becoming part of the district of [Mokotów](/wiki/Mokot%C3%B3w "Mokotów").[[17]](#cite_note-may51-17) In 1994, the area became part of new district of [Wilanów](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w "Wilanów"), with a small portion to the west of Opieńki Street becoming part of [Ursynów](/wiki/Ursyn%C3%B3w "Ursynów"), now forming the neighbourhood of Skarpa Powsińska.[[18]](#cite_note-gawyszewski1-18)[[14]](#cite_note-msiu-14)

In 1974, to the east of Powsiny was established the Polish Academy of Sciences Botanical Garden and Powsin Centre for Biological Diversity Conservation, with an area of 40 ha. It was developed between 1978 and 1990. It also incorporated the Janówek Villa, and its gardens, dating to 1913.[[19]](#cite_note-garden1-19)[[20]](#cite_note-fragor1-20) Currently, it is part of the City Information System area of Skarpa Powsińska.[[14]](#cite_note-msiu-14)

In 2006, Wilanów was subdivided into eight areas of the City Information System, a municipal standardized system of street signage, with Powsin becoming one of them.[[21]](#cite_note-msi-21)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Powsin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/2019_Warszawa_Przycz%C3%B3%C5%82kowa%2C_1.jpg/250px-2019_Warszawa_Przycz%C3%B3%C5%82kowa%2C_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:2019_Warszawa_Przycz%C3%B3%C5%82kowa,_1.jpg)

Single-family housing in Powsin at Przyczółkowa Street

The area features low-rise single-family housing and farmlands, with neighbourhoods of Powsin, [Kępa Latoszkowa](/wiki/K%C4%99pa_Latoszkowa "Kępa Latoszkowa"), [Latoszki](/wiki/Latoszki "Latoszki"), [Lisy](/wiki/Lisy,_Warsaw "Lisy, Warsaw"), [Stare Kabaty](/wiki/Stare_Kabaty "Stare Kabaty"), and [Zamość](/w/index.php?title=Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87,_Warsaw&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zamość, Warsaw (page does not exist)").[[4]](#cite_note-ew-4)

At 31 Przyczółkowa Street stands the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") St. Elizabeth Church, dating to the 18th century.[[5]](#cite_note-arch-5) Within the neighbourhood are also located two small necropoli. Nearby are also placed the Powsin Cemetery and the Warsaw Insurgents Cemetery.[[16]](#cite_note-pap-16)[[8]](#cite_note-cemetery-8)

The area includes lakes Lisy, Pod Morgami, Struga, and Torfowiska, as well as ditches Latoszka, Natolin, and Powsin.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)
